Intro Comments Hi all, thanks for producing this nice bit of software, I can see it becoming widely used for light curve extraction. The code is well documented with a few nice examples. I've split up my comments below into the relevant sections. ##### Installation: Installation was a little difficult for me, and took about an hour of troubleshooting to sort out (not the authors fault!). You can see above that I opened a PR regarding the problem, but prior to that the Python environment (3.8) I created was not supported. I think it would be useful to mention supported Python versions and a workaround for the above problem in the docs. ##### Getting started & Tutorials: All work fine with no issue. It would be nice to have some of the text explain what's happening when the quality test fails. e.g., the first example fails 2 quality tests, how come? Should we be worried? Can we force the light curve to extract anyway? The getting started tutorial has a typo in the link at the bottom of the page. There are quite a few typos in the docs in general (e.g., https://elk.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/diagnostic_gif.html has aperature, peack, identttify, etc). The image for the diagnostic_gif is missing. ##### General paper comments: The paper is nicely written and clearly explains the niche of integrated light curves. However, I think the paper is missing a short discussion of other light curve extraction software (e.g., eleanor). Indeed, looking at the source code shows that this method relies strongly on lightkurves _RegressionCorrector_ -- this should be mentioned more clearly in the text! Right now the text strongly implies that the `elk` is doing "singular value decomposition methods and principle component analysis" from scratch, but that is not the case. Finally, it's not unusual for JOSS papers to be illustrated with a figure. I know there's a lot of nice figures in the AAS version of this submission, but it might be worthwhile having a simple plot of an extracted light curve. This is totally up to the authors. Great work all, I'm looking forward to seeing more results come from this tool!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/1707496956/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

It doesn't seem to want to output the latest paper version. In any case, @ashleychontos, many thanks for addressing the majority of our issues. I do believe that unit tests are a crucial aspect of software development, more so for what I expect to be a widely used package. Right now, it looks like PySyd has a tests/ folder, but these are copied from another project. Regardless of how development proceeds, these copied tests should be deleted to prevent confusion. I copy below the JOSS guidelines on testing: ``` - Good: An automated test suite hooked up to continuous integration (GitHub Actions, Circle CI, or similar) - OK: Documented manual steps that can be followed to objectively check the expected functionality of the software (e.g., a sample input file to assert behavior) - Bad (not acceptable): No way for you, the reviewer, to objectively assess whether the software works ``` Right now, I see PySyd testing being between OK and Bad. The setup script and example gives us a manual test. But really, all the required functionality is here for automated testing: you can run these scripts (`pysyd run --star 1435467 -dv --ux 5000 --mc 200` etc) inside a test and assert that the output matches what you expect. What I (and Ben!) really want is the ability to clone the repo, `cd` to the `tests/` folder, run `pytest`, and get a simple pass/fail output. The link that Ben posted is a great resource to understand this. Otherwise, great work on the software. I want to commend the authors for such a comprehensive documentation, including a glossary(!) and library of related software. Thanks for inviting me to review `tomso`. This is a nice piece of software and a solid JOSS submission that will bridge the gap between the different stellar evolution code models. I've been playing around with the code for a while and it all runs nicely. My only issues relate to some lacking documentation and comparisons to other similar codes. I discuss these below. ### Installation and docs - Both pip install and from source works fine on a fresh Python 3.7 environment. - I am a bit alarmed at the line in the introduction that says the code is currently very unstable and the API will change drastically. Has this already happened? - There are community guidelines on the repository but not in the documentation. I don't think this is hugely important, but it's nice to have a short section after the installation. There should also be a small example on running the unit tests (which isn't always obvious!). - It would be nice to have some examples for the STARS models -- its inclusion in the Code Outputs page currently seems like an afterthought. - On that note, is STARS included in the Stellar Models page? I must admit I am unfamiliar with these models, but it looks like `tomso` can not convert between them? ### Unit tests: - The unit tests are nicely done and comprehensive. This is a great example of using GitHub workflows to automate testing -- nice work! This is only a suggestion, but it would be nice to have a badge on the repo showing the test status (i.e., guide [here](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/monitoring-and-troubleshooting-workflows/adding-a-workflow-status-badge)) ### Repository: - All good. ### Examples: - I get the same output running the examples in the documentation. - I do feel that the documentation is slightly lacking in some examples. It would be nice to have a few more examples for the command line interface and stellar models pages. Manuscript: - The manuscript is clear and concise. - My only issue here is that no discussion is made of similar packages (i.e., [PyGyre](https://github.com/rhdtownsend/pygyre)) and how they differ. Obviously `tomso` fills a different role, but there is some overlap in capabilities. - Some Googling around shows a few similar packages for reading in model data, such as [PyMesa](https://github.com/rjfarmer/pyMesa) and the [mesa_web](http://www.astro.wisc.edu/~townsend/static.php?ref=mesa-web-output) script.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/1119114689/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
